p.a:
This work is licensed under a CC
Attribution 4.0 International License

presentation.title: OpenClaw & Universal Agents: Why API-First Is Now an Agent Requirement
presentation.author: Frank Kilcommins & Erik Wilde

img.title: OpenAPI Initiative
img.style: float: right ; clear : both ; width : 30% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ;

ul.li: Head of Enterprise Architecture at Jentic
Governance Board Member at the OpenAPI Initiative
Co-Maintainer of the Arazzo Specification

img.title: OpenAPI Initiative
img.style: float: right ; clear : both ; width : 30% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ;

ul.li: Head of Enterprise Strategy at Jentic
Ambassador for the OpenAPI Initiative (OAI)

ol.li: A persistent, self-improving AI agent — runs 24/7 with memory, skills, and direct access to your tools, APIs, and CLIs
Multi-channel and multi-agent — connects across messaging platforms, orchestrates parallel agent workflows, and accumulates context across every interaction
API-native by design — consumes APIs directly without middleware layers, representing a new class of always-on, autonomous machine consumer
Beyond task-specific bots — universal agents discover, plan, and execute across any API or tool at runtime, without pre-wiring or human configuration
Machine-driven consumption at scale — APIs built for human developers now face AI consumers that operate faster, at higher volume, and with zero tribal knowledge
The shift changes everything — what was "good enough" for developers is fundamentally insufficient for autonomous agents: the API estate either enables the agentic future or blocks it

img.style: float: right ; height : 60% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ; filter: drop-shadow(0px 0px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) ;

ol.li: MCP is an entry, but it's really APIs all the way down
OpenClaw prefers direct API / CLI access - the shortest path

slide.title: Most API Value Creation is Indirect

ol.li: Using APIs to become better at internal reuse and cross-pollination
Reducing friction by having teams communicate via APIs
Using API First as a way to build everything as digital building blocks

ul.li: 2000s: Having any API is good
2010s: Having many APIs is good
2020s: Having good APIs is good
Little knowledge of API patterns and practices
Discovering APIs at run-time for solving tasks
Navigating only machine-readable parts of the API landscape
AI agents need information developers don’t typically require:
Everything that developers usually just know
Treat every API like an external one for developers with no context
OpenAPI Initiative (OAI) might add AX-specific fields
Until then, leverage description fields strategically
Discoverable: able to find and classify API
Interpretable: intent must be explicit
Composable: clear inputs / outputs
Safe: authNZ & safety signals machine readable
Predictable: idempotency, stable responses
Resilient: clear signals for success and failure

slide.title: You Can't Improve What You Can't Measure
slide.p: AI-readiness starts with understanding your current API landscape

slide.title: API AI-Readiness Framework
slide.p: Evaluates APIs across six human & AI-readiness dimensions

ul.li: Evaluates if an API is structurally valid, standards-compliance, and parseable by tooling.

th.style: background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;

tr.td: Checks whether the API parses as a valid OpenAPI
What proportion of $ref references successfully resolve
Aggregated quality score from linter diagnostics, weighted by severity
Measures whether the API's underlying data model is coherent enough for automated reasoning (semantic or logical defects that impact reliable interpretation)

ul.li: Evaluates how easily AI systems can locate, classify, and route to the API across registries, workflows, and knowledge bases.

th.style: background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;
background-color: #385155; color: #ffffff; font-weight: bold; text-align: left;

tr.td: Evaluates the semantic value of textual descriptions within an API description
Evaluates verb-object clarity of summaries and descriptions
Evaluates indicators to workflow specifics including Arazzo/MCP/workflow references. Also analyses existence of callbacks etc.
Detects metadata related to API gateways, llms.txt, APIs.json, MCP registries, externalDocs links to Dev portals
Detect domain/taxonomy classification through the use of tags

slide.title: API Discoverability - Do it for the Agents (an your teams)!

ul.li: Better Signal Scores = Better APIs = Better Searchability
Improved results in vanilla and specialized search offerings
Using expensive models doesn't fix bad API semantics
Save yourself the trouble and the $$$
